comments:
Summary:
This paper studies stochastic K-armed contextual bandits under generalized linear models (GLMs). It proposes two UCB-style algorithms: UCB-GLM, a practical algorithm with an Õ(d √ T) regret bound, and SupCB-GLM, which attains an (near) optimal Õ(√(dT) log K) regret for finite K. A core technical contribution is a new non-asymptotic, direction-wise (normality-type) confidence bound for the MLE in GLMs (Theorem 1). The analysis leverages self-normalized martingale bounds and an independence-construction technique from Auer (2003). The paper discusses algorithmic complexity and lists open problems.

Strengths:
- Important problem: GLM contextual bandits are highly relevant in practice (e.g., logistic rewards) and theory has lagged behind linear bandits. 
- Strong theoretical contributions: the finite-sample normality-type bound for GLM MLEs is novel and of independent interest. 
- Improved regret guarantees: the paper improves prior Õ(d √ T) results (Filippi et al., 2010) by removing an extra √d factor for small K and matches minimax lower bounds up to logs. 
- Clear connection to and comparison with prior work (Filippi et al., Auer, EXP4, etc.). The paper also honestly discusses limitations and open questions (computational cost, dependence on K in lower bounds, randomized methods).

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Some results require nontrivial technical conditions (e.g., lower bounds on λmin(Vn) in Theorem 1 and related propositions). While the paper gives sufficient conditions and heuristics, the practical applicability when these conditions fail is not fully characterized. 
- Computational cost: both algorithms require solving MLEs using O(t) past samples and inverting d×d matrices; while the authors acknowledge this and suggest first-order methods, empirical evidence or concrete scalable variants are not provided. 
- The high-probability bounds involve many tuning parameters (α, τ, δ) and somewhat complex constants; more discussion/guidance on parameter choices in practice would be helpful. 
- The provided text contains some typographical/formatting noise (likely OCR issues) which slightly hampers readability, although the mathematical exposition itself is generally coherent.

Soundness and technical assessment:
The paper provides clear proof sketches and pointers to detailed proofs in appendices. The main theorems are plausible and appear to follow from standard martingale/self-normalized concentration tools combined with the new finite-sample analysis of GLM MLEs. The authors carefully state assumptions (smoothness and local bounded derivative of link function, sub-Gaussian noise, feature boundedness) and quantify regime requirements (sample size, eigenvalue lower bounds). Given the material provided, I find the technical approach convincing, though some bounds rely on spectral conditions that deserve more discussion regarding when they hold in realistic contexts.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Provide experiments demonstrating empirical performance and sensitivity to tuning parameters and to violations of the λmin conditions. 
- Explore computationally efficient implementations (online/iterative MLE updates, sketching, or stochastic approximation) and analyze their regret trade-offs. 
- Discuss the tightness of constants and whether the extra log factors can be removed.
- Expand discussion on possible K-dependence in lower bounds and on randomized algorithms such as Thompson sampling for GLMs.

Recommendation:
This paper makes an important theoretical advance for GLM contextual bandits, with a novel finite-sample normality result and near-optimal regret algorithms. I recommend acceptance and consider it suitable for an oral presentation.
